New Zealand History
SIGNIFICANCE OF THE TREATY OF WAITANGI | TE TIRITI O WAITANGI
heading_path: SIGNIFICANCE OF THE TREATY OF WAITANGI | TE TIRITI O WAITANGI

content_blocks.raw_text: The Treaty of Waitangi | te Tiriti o Waitangi is important as New Zealand’s founding agreement between the British Crown and Māori rangatira (chiefs).
heading_path: SIGNIFICANCE OF THE TREATY OF WAITANGI | TE TIRITI O WAITANGI

content_blocks.raw_text: The Treaty of Waitangi | te Tiriti o Waitangi was first signed on 6 February 1840 at Waitangi in the Bay of Islands.
heading_path: SIGNIFICANCE OF THE TREATY OF WAITANGI | TE TIRITI O WAITANGI

content_blocks.raw_text: The Treaty of Waitangi | te Tiriti o Waitangi has ongoing significance as a symbol of mutual respect, shared heritage, and commitment to working together.
heading_path: SIGNIFICANCE OF THE TREATY OF WAITANGI | TE TIRITI O WAITANGI

content_blocks.raw_text: There are two documents: the Treaty of Waitangi in English and te Tiriti o Waitangi in te reo Māori.
heading_path: SIGNIFICANCE OF THE TREATY OF WAITANGI | TE TIRITI O WAITANGI

content_blocks.raw_text: James Busby acted as the British Resident in New Zealand from 1833. He played a role in developing the first New Zealand flag in 1834 (United Tribes flag) and helped to facilitate the drafting of the Treaty of Waitangi in 1840.
heading_path: SIGNIFICANCE OF THE TREATY OF WAITANGI | TE TIRITI O WAITANGI

content_blocks.raw_text: William Hobson was the first Governor of New Zealand. He proposed a treaty with Māori to establish British sovereignty.
heading_path: SIGNIFICANCE OF THE TREATY OF WAITANGI | TE TIRITI O WAITANGI

content_blocks.raw_text: Henry Williams translated the Treaty of Waitangi into te reo Māori with his son Edward and explained it to Māori chiefs. As the leader of the Church Missionary Society in New Zealand, he contributed to the spread of Christianity among Māori and expanded colonial settlement. He also attempted to prevent hostilities at Kororāreka in 1845.
heading_path: SIGNIFICANCE OF THE TREATY OF WAITANGI | TE TIRITI O WAITANGI

content_blocks.raw_text: Hōne Heke was the first Māori chief to sign the Treaty of Waitangi in 1840. Later, he led Māori resistance against British colonial authority over Treaty breaches and to protect rangatiratanga. He ordered the cutting of the British flagstaff at Kororāreka four times between 1844 and 1845, sparking the Northern War.
heading_path: SIGNIFICANCE OF THE TREATY OF WAITANGI | TE TIRITI O WAITANGI

content_blocks.raw_text: NATURAL EVENTS OF HISTORICAL SIGNIFICANCE IN NEW ZEALAND
heading_path: NATURAL EVENTS OF HISTORICAL SIGNIFICANCE IN NEW ZEALAND

content_blocks.raw_text: Mt Tarawera
heading_path: NATURAL EVENTS OF HISTORICAL SIGNIFICANCE IN NEW ZEALAND

content_blocks.raw_text: Pink and White Terraces.
heading_path: NATURAL EVENTS OF HISTORICAL SIGNIFICANCE IN NEW ZEALAND

content_blocks.raw_text: Promotion of New Zealand landscapes to Victorian travellers. An early site of tourism.
heading_path: NATURAL EVENTS OF HISTORICAL SIGNIFICANCE IN NEW ZEALAND

content_blocks.raw_text: The eruption (1886) and impacts:
heading_path: NATURAL EVENTS OF HISTORICAL SIGNIFICANCE IN NEW ZEALAND

content_blocks.raw_text: Lasted six hours and caused massive destruction.
heading_path: NATURAL EVENTS OF HISTORICAL SIGNIFICANCE IN NEW ZEALAND

content_blocks.raw_text: Destroyed a number of villages and buried the Pink and White Terraces.
heading_path: NATURAL EVENTS OF HISTORICAL SIGNIFICANCE IN NEW ZEALAND

content_blocks.raw_text: Devastating impact through loss of life; loss of landscape. Impact on the livelihoods of Māori — guiding and tourism.
heading_path: NATURAL EVENTS OF HISTORICAL SIGNIFICANCE IN NEW ZEALAND

content_blocks.raw_text: Interpretations of the events at the time: phantom canoe, Sophia Hinerangi, Tuhoto Ariki.
heading_path: NATURAL EVENTS OF HISTORICAL SIGNIFICANCE IN NEW ZEALAND

content_blocks.raw_text: Subsequent development (1931) of Buried Village of Te Wairoa as a tourist attraction and archaeological site.
heading_path: NATURAL EVENTS OF HISTORICAL SIGNIFICANCE IN NEW ZEALAND

content_blocks.raw_text: A case study building on Year 5 Geography
heading_path: NATURAL EVENTS OF HISTORICAL SIGNIFICANCE IN NEW ZEALAND

content_blocks.raw_text: Teachers choose one of the following as a case study with a focus on recovery and regeneration:
heading_path: NATURAL EVENTS OF HISTORICAL SIGNIFICANCE IN NEW ZEALAND

content_blocks.raw_text: Hawke’s Bay earthquake (1931)
heading_path: NATURAL EVENTS OF HISTORICAL SIGNIFICANCE IN NEW ZEALAND

content_blocks.raw_text: Tangiwai Disaster (1953)
heading_path: NATURAL EVENTS OF HISTORICAL SIGNIFICANCE IN NEW ZEALAND

content_blocks.raw_text: Cyclone Giselle (1968)
heading_path: NATURAL EVENTS OF HISTORICAL SIGNIFICANCE IN NEW ZEALAND

content_blocks.raw_text: Southland Floods (1984)
heading_path: NATURAL EVENTS OF HISTORICAL SIGNIFICANCE IN NEW ZEALAND

content_blocks.raw_text: Cyclone Bola (1988)
heading_path: NATURAL EVENTS OF HISTORICAL SIGNIFICANCE IN NEW ZEALAND

content_blocks.raw_text: Canterbury earthquake (2011)
heading_path: NATURAL EVENTS OF HISTORICAL SIGNIFICANCE IN NEW ZEALAND

content_blocks.raw_text: West Coast Floods (2022)
heading_path: NATURAL EVENTS OF HISTORICAL SIGNIFICANCE IN NEW ZEALAND

content_blocks.raw_text: Newspaper articles, first-hand accounts, and photographic evidence can enrich this study.
heading_path: NATURAL EVENTS OF HISTORICAL SIGNIFICANCE IN NEW ZEALAND

content_blocks.raw_text: NEW ZEALAND TO 1840
heading_path: NEW ZEALAND TO 1840

content_blocks.raw_text: Impact, including Māori response, of early European contact on:
heading_path: NEW ZEALAND TO 1840

content_blocks.raw_text: Trade and settlement
heading_path: NEW ZEALAND TO 1840

content_blocks.raw_text: Sealing and whaling (from the 1790s, port towns and trading posts, Māori–Pākehā individuals like Barnet Burns).
heading_path: NEW ZEALAND TO 1840

content_blocks.raw_text: Māori provision of timber, flax, and food in exchange for muskets, iron goods, and blankets.
heading_path: NEW ZEALAND TO 1840

content_blocks.raw_text: Impact of new diseases (measles, influenza) on Māori population.
heading_path: NEW ZEALAND TO 1840

content_blocks.raw_text: Missionaries, literacy, and cultural exchange
heading_path: NEW ZEALAND TO 1840

content_blocks.raw_text: Arrival of Christian missionaries: provision of European farming tools, healthcare, and schooling.
heading_path: NEW ZEALAND TO 1840

content_blocks.raw_text: Establishment of a te reo Māori press, distribution of translations, and rapid growth of literacy.
heading_path: NEW ZEALAND TO 1840

content_blocks.raw_text: Māori adaptation of Christian ideas alongside traditional Indigenous spiritual practices.
heading_path: NEW ZEALAND TO 1840

content_blocks.raw_text: Land ownership and sovereignty
heading_path: NEW ZEALAND TO 1840

content_blocks.raw_text: Petitions to King William IV and other Crown appeals by rangatira seeking protection, redress, or recognition.
heading_path: NEW ZEALAND TO 1840

content_blocks.raw_text: 1835 Declaration of Independence | He Whakaputanga signed by rangatira.
heading_path: NEW ZEALAND TO 1840

content_blocks.raw_text: Early land-purchase deeds (New Zealand Company), contrasting Māori concepts of use, tūrangawaewae, and mana versus the Crown’s notion of permanent sale.
heading_path: NEW ZEALAND TO 1840

content_blocks.raw_text: The Musket Wars
heading_path: NEW ZEALAND TO 1840

content_blocks.raw_text: Musket Wars (c.1810s–1830s): causes, major campaigns, and consequences for population movements and tribal boundaries. Hongi Hika’s role.
heading_path: NEW ZEALAND TO 1840

content_blocks.raw_text: Moriori
heading_path: NEW ZEALAND TO 1840

content_blocks.raw_text: The expression of mana through Nunuku’s Law and the renunciation of violence even in the face of great external challenges (the arrival of Europeans from the 1790s and of Ngāti Tama and Ngāti Mutunga from 1835).
heading_path: NEW ZEALAND TO 1840

content_blocks.raw_text: For example, consider: examples of different ways that people commemorate Waitangi Day across New Zealand why the Treaty of Waitangi | te Tiriti o Waitangi is important to public life in New Zealand today how information can be collected about the signing of the Treaty of Waitangi | te Tiriti o Waitangi
heading_path: NEW ZEALAND TO 1840

content_blocks.raw_text: For example, consider: how Māori social, economic, and cultural practices changed following the Tarawera eruption the consequences on trade and settlement the changes to population and environments over the period how legislation and town planning evolved followed natural events how sources such as newspaper articles, first-hand accounts, and photographic evidence can be used to reconstruct historical narratives how historical documents and artefacts can be studied to understand how and why societies and events change over time
heading_path: NEW ZEALAND TO 1840

content_blocks.raw_text: For example, consider: how Māori social, economic, and cultural practices changed with the arrival of European settlers the consequences of European arrival on trade and settlement the causes of the Musket Wars the consequences of missionaries, literacy, and cultural exchange on Māori society the changes to population and environments over the period Global History
heading_path: NEW ZEALAND TO 1840

content_blocks.raw_text: DYNASTIES IN ANCIENT AND IMPERIAL CHINA, C.2100–1912 CE
heading_path: DYNASTIES IN ANCIENT AND IMPERIAL CHINA, C.2100–1912 CE

content_blocks.raw_text: Shang Dynasty
heading_path: DYNASTIES IN ANCIENT AND IMPERIAL CHINA, C.2100–1912 CE

content_blocks.raw_text: Where and when?
heading_path: DYNASTIES IN ANCIENT AND IMPERIAL CHINA, C.2100–1912 CE

content_blocks.raw_text: Yellow River valley (northern China).
heading_path: DYNASTIES IN ANCIENT AND IMPERIAL CHINA, C.2100–1912 CE

content_blocks.raw_text: About 1600–1046 BCE.
heading_path: DYNASTIES IN ANCIENT AND IMPERIAL CHINA, C.2100–1912 CE

content_blocks.raw_text: Bronze Age.
heading_path: DYNASTIES IN ANCIENT AND IMPERIAL CHINA, C.2100–1912 CE

content_blocks.raw_text: Farming
heading_path: DYNASTIES IN ANCIENT AND IMPERIAL CHINA, C.2100–1912 CE

content_blocks.raw_text: Flood control and millet/rice paddy fields — building ditches and fields held back floods. Creation of reliable agricultural surpluses for towns.
heading_path: DYNASTIES IN ANCIENT AND IMPERIAL CHINA, C.2100–1912 CE

content_blocks.raw_text: Power and rulers (government)
heading_path: DYNASTIES IN ANCIENT AND IMPERIAL CHINA, C.2100–1912 CE

content_blocks.raw_text: Hereditary kings directed religious rituals although not considered divine. Made law, raised taxes, led armies. Leaders in regions helped the king govern.
heading_path: DYNASTIES IN ANCIENT AND IMPERIAL CHINA, C.2100–1912 CE

content_blocks.raw_text: People and daily life
heading_path: DYNASTIES IN ANCIENT AND IMPERIAL CHINA, C.2100–1912 CE

content_blocks.raw_text: Social hierarchy: king → priests → warriors → artisans → farmers → slaves.
heading_path: DYNASTIES IN ANCIENT AND IMPERIAL CHINA, C.2100–1912 CE

content_blocks.raw_text: Timber houses on raised earth platforms — homes built up high stayed dry in floods and kept people safe. Clan-based villages.
heading_path: DYNASTIES IN ANCIENT AND IMPERIAL CHINA, C.2100–1912 CE

content_blocks.raw_text: Trade
heading_path: DYNASTIES IN ANCIENT AND IMPERIAL CHINA, C.2100–1912 CE

content_blocks.raw_text: Used cowrie shells as currency; traded bronze, jade, and silk were traded across long distances on the silk road.
heading_path: DYNASTIES IN ANCIENT AND IMPERIAL CHINA, C.2100–1912 CE

content_blocks.raw_text: Religious beliefs and practices
heading_path: DYNASTIES IN ANCIENT AND IMPERIAL CHINA, C.2100–1912 CE

content_blocks.raw_text: Ancestor worship and bronze ding vessels.
heading_path: DYNASTIES IN ANCIENT AND IMPERIAL CHINA, C.2100–1912 CE

content_blocks.raw_text: Oracle-bone inscriptions.
heading_path: DYNASTIES IN ANCIENT AND IMPERIAL CHINA, C.2100–1912 CE

content_blocks.raw_text: Writing and other inventions Bronze casting.
heading_path: DYNASTIES IN ANCIENT AND IMPERIAL CHINA, C.2100–1912 CE

content_blocks.raw_text: Chariots with spoked wheels and yokes Silk weaving and hemp textiles.
heading_path: DYNASTIES IN ANCIENT AND IMPERIAL CHINA, C.2100–1912 CE

content_blocks.raw_text: COMPARATIVE STUDY: THE ELIZABETHAN AND VICTORIAN AGES
heading_path: COMPARATIVE STUDY: THE ELIZABETHAN AND VICTORIAN AGES

content_blocks.raw_text: The Elizabethan age
heading_path: COMPARATIVE STUDY: THE ELIZABETHAN AND VICTORIAN AGES

content_blocks.raw_text: Life for different groups in society
heading_path: COMPARATIVE STUDY: THE ELIZABETHAN AND VICTORIAN AGES

content_blocks.raw_text: Nobility and gentry, merchants, farmers, labourers, and beggars and destitute. Role of women.
heading_path: COMPARATIVE STUDY: THE ELIZABETHAN AND VICTORIAN AGES

content_blocks.raw_text: Children: education mostly for boys of higher status; many worked from young age. Around 30% of boys and 10% of girls learnt to read.
heading_path: COMPARATIVE STUDY: THE ELIZABETHAN AND VICTORIAN AGES

content_blocks.raw_text: Monarch’s power versus power of Parliament or the people
heading_path: COMPARATIVE STUDY: THE ELIZABETHAN AND VICTORIAN AGES

content_blocks.raw_text: Elizabeth I ruled England from 1558 to 1603.
heading_path: COMPARATIVE STUDY: THE ELIZABETHAN AND VICTORIAN AGES

content_blocks.raw_text: Government: The monarch seen as the main ruler, an ‘absolute monarch’, with ‘government’ meaning the queen and her officials.
heading_path: COMPARATIVE STUDY: THE ELIZABETHAN AND VICTORIAN AGES

content_blocks.raw_text: Parliament as a group of powerful men who helped make laws and grant taxes and did not represent ordinary people. Parliament’s limited power to challenge the queen. Most people had no say in choosing its members.
heading_path: COMPARATIVE STUDY: THE ELIZABETHAN AND VICTORIAN AGES

content_blocks.raw_text: Religion
heading_path: COMPARATIVE STUDY: THE ELIZABETHAN AND VICTORIAN AGES

content_blocks.raw_text: Protestant religious rules and practices. How they were enforced across England and the treatment of people who continued to follow Catholic beliefs.
heading_path: COMPARATIVE STUDY: THE ELIZABETHAN AND VICTORIAN AGES

content_blocks.raw_text: Trade and exploration and changes in buildings, technology, or inventions and culture
heading_path: COMPARATIVE STUDY: THE ELIZABETHAN AND VICTORIAN AGES

content_blocks.raw_text: Growth of exploration and overseas ventures and privateering, including links with the Americas and Africa.
heading_path: COMPARATIVE STUDY: THE ELIZABETHAN AND VICTORIAN AGES

content_blocks.raw_text: Invention of the printing press in 1485. Growth of printing. Influence of Renaissance and works of William Shakespeare.
heading_path: COMPARATIVE STUDY: THE ELIZABETHAN AND VICTORIAN AGES

content_blocks.raw_text: The Victorian age
heading_path: COMPARATIVE STUDY: THE ELIZABETHAN AND VICTORIAN AGES

content_blocks.raw_text: Life for different groups in British society
heading_path: COMPARATIVE STUDY: THE ELIZABETHAN AND VICTORIAN AGES

content_blocks.raw_text: Features of upper, middle, and working classes and underclass of destitute. Role of women.
heading_path: COMPARATIVE STUDY: THE ELIZABETHAN AND VICTORIAN AGES

content_blocks.raw_text: Children: some worked in harsh conditions; access to schooling improved. By the end of Victoria’s reign, nearly the entire population could read and write.
heading_path: COMPARATIVE STUDY: THE ELIZABETHAN AND VICTORIAN AGES

content_blocks.raw_text: Steady rise in average wages across the population during Queen Victoria’s reign, with more jobs and greater availability of everyday goods in shops (e.g. soap, matches, clothes, newspapers, toys).
heading_path: COMPARATIVE STUDY: THE ELIZABETHAN AND VICTORIAN AGES

content_blocks.raw_text: Monarch’s power versus power of Parliament or the people
heading_path: COMPARATIVE STUDY: THE ELIZABETHAN AND VICTORIAN AGES

content_blocks.raw_text: Queen Victoria was monarch of the United Kingdom from 1837 to 1901.
heading_path: COMPARATIVE STUDY: THE ELIZABETHAN AND VICTORIAN AGES

content_blocks.raw_text: Queen Victoria did not have a role in daily decisions of government. Limited political power in a constitutional monarchy, with decisions made by elected government.
heading_path: COMPARATIVE STUDY: THE ELIZABETHAN AND VICTORIAN AGES

content_blocks.raw_text: Government as prime minister and cabinet now drawn from the majority grouping of members of Parliament (MPs) in Parliament. Parliament still made up of the House of Lords and House of Commons.
heading_path: COMPARATIVE STUDY: THE ELIZABETHAN AND VICTORIAN AGES

content_blocks.raw_text: Expanding voting rights, although working-class people remained excluded for much of the reign. No women could vote.
heading_path: COMPARATIVE STUDY: THE ELIZABETHAN AND VICTORIAN AGES

content_blocks.raw_text: Religion
heading_path: COMPARATIVE STUDY: THE ELIZABETHAN AND VICTORIAN AGES

content_blocks.raw_text: Largely Christian society with emergence of scientific challenge (e.g. Darwin’s theory of evolution).
heading_path: COMPARATIVE STUDY: THE ELIZABETHAN AND VICTORIAN AGES

content_blocks.raw_text: Trade and exploration and changes in buildings, technology, and inventions
heading_path: COMPARATIVE STUDY: THE ELIZABETHAN AND VICTORIAN AGES

content_blocks.raw_text: Expansion of global empire and trade networks, with links to Africa, Asia, and Oceania, including New Zealand.
heading_path: COMPARATIVE STUDY: THE ELIZABETHAN AND VICTORIAN AGES

content_blocks.raw_text: Technological innovation, such as the telegraph, photography, gas lighting, and public sanitation systems. Rapid industrialisation.
heading_path: COMPARATIVE STUDY: THE ELIZABETHAN AND VICTORIAN AGES

content_blocks.raw_text: For example, consider: what Athens and other Greek city states had in common similarities and differences in the role played by religion in society between the two periods the sources we use to find out about Ancient Greece and why it is sometimes difficult to be certain about how people lived at the time how the Ancient Greek civilisation was similar to and different from the Egyptian civilisation the historical significance of Ancient Greece in shaping the modern world
heading_path: COMPARATIVE STUDY: THE ELIZABETHAN AND VICTORIAN AGES

content_blocks.raw_text: For example, consider: the differences between the different dynasties the sources we can use to find out about the Chinese religious beliefs and their societal impact the differences between the dynastic rule and other societal structures the societal impact of the Silk Road the historical significance of dynastic China in shaping the modern world
heading_path: COMPARATIVE STUDY: THE ELIZABETHAN AND VICTORIAN AGES

content_blocks.raw_text: For example, consider: the similarities and differences in the monarch’s power versus the power of Parliament and the people between the two periods how life varied for different groups in Elizabethan and Victorian society the different types of trade and extent of exploration between the two periods
heading_path: COMPARATIVE STUDY: THE ELIZABETHAN AND VICTORIAN AGES

content_blocks.raw_text: Government and society: The Roman Republic:

heading_path: ROME FROM REPUBLIC TO EMPIRE

content_blocks.raw_text: What is meant by a republic and how power was held and exercised in the Roman Republic.
heading_path: ROME FROM REPUBLIC TO EMPIRE

content_blocks.raw_text: Roman laws.
heading_path: ROME FROM REPUBLIC TO EMPIRE

content_blocks.raw_text: Roman army: training, armour, legions, what made them strong.
heading_path: ROME FROM REPUBLIC TO EMPIRE

content_blocks.raw_text: Roman emperors and how they ruled the Roman Empire
heading_path: ROME FROM REPUBLIC TO EMPIRE

content_blocks.raw_text: Why Rome stopped being a republic.
heading_path: ROME FROM REPUBLIC TO EMPIRE

content_blocks.raw_text: How rule by an emperor was different from rule by Senate in the Republic.
heading_path: ROME FROM REPUBLIC TO EMPIRE

content_blocks.raw_text: Roman religion
heading_path: ROME FROM REPUBLIC TO EMPIRE

content_blocks.raw_text: Roman gods and goddesses.
heading_path: ROME FROM REPUBLIC TO EMPIRE

content_blocks.raw_text: Festivals and temples (e.g. Saturnalia).
heading_path: ROME FROM REPUBLIC TO EMPIRE

content_blocks.raw_text: Roads, cities, and everyday life
heading_path: ROME FROM REPUBLIC TO EMPIRE

content_blocks.raw_text: Roman roads, baths, aqueducts, temples, forums.
heading_path: ROME FROM REPUBLIC TO EMPIRE

content_blocks.raw_text: Clothing, food, entertainment (gladiators, theatre).
heading_path: ROME FROM REPUBLIC TO EMPIRE

content_blocks.raw_text: The role of slaves in Roman society.
heading_path: ROME FROM REPUBLIC TO EMPIRE

content_blocks.raw_text: For example, consider: the differences between monarchical rule (e.g. Egyptian Pharaohs or Roman kings) and the Roman Republic the reasons why the Roman Republic ended the similarities and differences between Roman religion and beliefs in Ancient Egypt and Ancient Greece the sources we can use to find out about Romans’ religious beliefs the historical significance of the Roman Empire in shaping the modern world.
heading_path: ROME FROM REPUBLIC TO EMPIRE
